---
title: "Permutation Tests: Testing Without a Model"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # observed data / sample
OBS    = "#0a7d4d"     # the observed statistic / highlight
PERM   = "#d6791f"     # permutation / null-by-relabeling quantities
REJECT = "#c1121f"     # tail / rejection
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # histograms / shaded areas

rng = np.random.default_rng(20240917)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the third article of movement E —
> *computational and alternative inference* — of the **Statistical Inference** track, and
> it closes the resampling pair. It assumes two earlier articles, each built from the
> ground up. From [INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*] it takes the entire
> apparatus of a test: a **null hypothesis** $H_0$, a **test statistic** $T$, a **null
> distribution** against which $T$ is judged, and the **p-value** as a tail probability
> *computed under the null* — together with the long list of what a p-value is **not**.
> From [INFR-E1 *The Bootstrap*] it takes the resampling instinct — that when you cannot
> *derive* a distribution you can *simulate* it — and the crucial contrast we will sharpen
> here: the bootstrap resamples **with replacement** to mimic *drawing a fresh sample*,
> whereas the permutation test reshuffles labels **without replacement** to mimic *the
> null's own symmetry*. From the Probability Foundations track it borrows the
> **combinatorics of permutations and combinations** [PROB-04], which we restate where we
> use them. Where the earlier tests of movement D ([INFR-D2 *The Workhorse Tests*]) bought
> their null distributions with an assumption — Normality, or a large-sample
> approximation — this article shows how to **manufacture an exact null distribution from
> the data alone**, assuming only a symmetry the null itself supplies. It **sets up** the
> permutation-based control of family-wise error in [INFR-D5 *Multiple Comparisons*] and
> stands as the assumption-light companion to every two-sample test in the track.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

When the null hypothesis says "the labels do not matter," it hands us something
remarkable: under that null, **any relabeling of the data is just as probable as the one
we observed**. That is the property of **exchangeability**, and it is enough to build a
hypothesis test out of nothing but counting. We compute our test statistic on the real
data, then on every relabeling the null treats as equivalent; the fraction of relabelings
that match or beat the observed value *is* the p-value — exact, distribution-free, and
requiring no Normal curve, no $t$-table, no large-$n$ limit. This article derives the
permutation test from its single load-bearing idea (exchangeability under $H_0$), proves
that the resulting test is **exactly level-$\alpha$** in finite samples, and shows why
enumerating all $\binom{N}{n}$ relabelings is usually replaced by a **Monte-Carlo**
estimate — deriving the correct $(b+1)/(B+1)$ formula and explaining the otherwise
mysterious "$+1$." We carry three honest examples end to end: a two-sample difference of
means on clinical data, a test of correlation by permuting one column, and a paired design
where exchangeability lives *within* pairs (sign-flipping), not across them. We show the
permutation test agrees with the two-sample $t$-test under Normality and beats it under
heavy tails — and then walk to the **edge of applicability**, where exchangeability fails
(unequal nuisance variances, dependence, the Behrens–Fisher problem) and the test silently
loses its guarantee. The single claim the article defends is that **a permutation test is
only as valid as the exchangeability it assumes** — get that right and you have the most
honest test in statistics; get it wrong and you have a confident answer to the wrong
question.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Suppose the null hypothesis is a statement of **no effect** that makes the data
**exchangeable** — meaning every ordering, or every assignment of labels, is equally
likely to have produced what you saw. ("Treatment does nothing" makes the treatment and
control labels arbitrary; "$X$ and $Y$ are independent" makes the pairing of $x$'s with
$y$'s arbitrary.) Then build the null distribution *by brute force*:

> *Compute the test statistic $T_{\text{obs}}$ on the real data. Now relabel — shuffle the
> group labels, or shuffle one of the paired columns — and recompute $T$ on the shuffled
> data. Do this for all (or many random) relabelings. The collection of those values is
> the **exact null distribution** of $T$, because under $H_0$ each relabeling was equally
> probable. The p-value is the fraction of relabelings whose statistic is at least as
> extreme as $T_{\text{obs}}$.*

No assumption about the *shape* of the data is needed — no Normality, no large sample, no
formula for a standard error. The only assumption is the **exchangeability** the null
itself supplies. That is the whole method, and also the whole catch: the test is exactly
as trustworthy as that exchangeability claim.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The load-bearing word is **exchangeability**: it is the
hinge that turns "the null is true" into "all relabelings are equally likely," which is in
turn what lets us *count* our way to a null distribution instead of deriving one. We make
that hinge precise in @sec-formal, prove the test it builds is exactly level-$\alpha$ in
@sec-exactness, replace exhaustive counting with Monte Carlo (and get the "$+1$" right) in
@sec-monte-carlo, run three real examples in @sec-examples, and — the half that separates
competence from mastery — find exactly where exchangeability breaks in @sec-failure.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the idea {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **what do we test with when we are not
willing to assume a distribution?** Every move below answers a piece of that. If a step
feels like sleight of hand, return here.

## The original problem: every test in movement D borrowed a curve

Look back at the tests we have built. The one-sample $z$-test ([INFR-D2]) compares a
standardized mean to the standard Normal curve — and earns that curve either by *assuming*
the data are Normal or by invoking the Central Limit Theorem ([INFR-A2]) for large $n$.
The $t$-test trades the known-$\sigma$ Normal for Student's $t_{n-1}$, but only after
*assuming the data themselves are Normal* so that the sample variance has the chi-square
distribution the derivation needs ([INFR-C2]). The $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test leans on
a large-sample approximation that the expected cell counts be "big enough." Every one of
these tests answers "is this surprising under $H_0$?" by reading a tail area off a
**theoretical null distribution** — and every one of those theoretical distributions was
purchased with an assumption about the shape of the data or the size of the sample.

That is fine when the assumption holds. But consider the situations a working
statistician actually faces:

- A two-sample comparison with $n_1 = 6$ and $n_2 = 7$ — far too small for the CLT to
  rescue a non-Normal distribution, and you have no reason to believe the data are Normal.
- A test statistic with **no tractable null distribution at all** — the difference of two
  *medians*, a difference of two *trimmed means*, the ratio of two interquartile ranges.
  There is no "$t$-table" for the difference of medians.
- Data with one wild outlier, where the Normal-theory $t$-test's null distribution is
  simply the wrong shape and its p-value is a fiction.

In each case the machinery of movement D stalls, not because the *logic* of testing fails
— $H_0$, a statistic, a tail probability — but because the one ingredient we cannot
otherwise get, **the null distribution of the statistic**, is unavailable. We are missing
a curve, not a concept.

## The escape: let the null build its own distribution

Here is the idea, due in spirit to **R. A. Fisher** (1935) and made famous by his "lady
tasting tea," and put on a general footing by **E. J. G. Pitman** (1937–38). Fisher's
insight was that for certain null hypotheses you do not *need* a theoretical null
distribution, because the null itself tells you exactly how the data could have come out
differently. If a treatment truly does nothing, then which subjects got labeled "treated"
and which "control" was, as far as the *outcomes* are concerned, a coin flip — the
outcomes would have been the same numbers regardless of the labels. So the data we
*could* have seen, under the null, are exactly the data we *did* see with the labels
reshuffled. Enumerate those reshufflings, compute the statistic on each, and you have the
null distribution — not approximated, but *constructed*, from the data.

This is the same resampling instinct as the bootstrap ([INFR-E1]) — *simulate what you
cannot derive* — but pointed at a different target and obeying a different rule, and the
contrast is worth stating now because the two are constantly confused.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the difference between the bootstrap and a permutation test? They both
resample."* Three sentences you should be able to give cleanly:

1. **They estimate different things.** The bootstrap estimates the **sampling
   distribution of an estimator** — its spread, its bias, its confidence interval — by
   mimicking *drawing a fresh sample from the population*. The permutation test estimates
   the **null distribution of a test statistic** by mimicking *the null hypothesis's own
   symmetry*.
2. **They resample differently.** The bootstrap draws **with replacement** (a fresh
   sample of size $n$ can repeat points), because that is what re-drawing from a
   population looks like. The permutation test reshuffles **without replacement** (it is a
   true rearrangement — every data value is used exactly once), because that is what
   "relabeling" means.
3. **They answer different questions.** Bootstrap $\to$ *how uncertain is my estimate?*
   Permutation $\to$ *could this effect be pure label-luck?* The bootstrap is built for
   intervals (movement E2); the permutation test is built for tests (movement D logic).

The deepest version of the answer: the permutation test is **exact** under exchangeability
— its Type I rate is exactly $\alpha$ in finite samples — while the bootstrap is generally
only **asymptotically** valid. That exactness is what we prove in @sec-exactness, and it
is the permutation test's signature advantage.
:::

# Intuition before symbols: the lady tasting tea {#sec-intuition}

Before any formula, the canonical picture. A lady claims she can taste whether the milk or
the tea was poured into the cup first. Fisher presents her with eight cups — four
milk-first, four tea-first, in random order — and asks her to identify the four
milk-first cups. She gets all four right.

Is that impressive, or luck? The null hypothesis is **she is guessing**: her labels carry
no information, so any assignment of her four "milk-first" guesses to the eight cups is
equally likely. Under that null we do not need a distribution from a book — we can *count*.
There are $\binom{8}{4} = 70$ ways to choose which four cups she calls "milk-first." Exactly
**one** of those 70 gets all four correct. So if she is guessing, the probability of a
perfect score is $1/70 \approx 0.014$. That number — the fraction of equally-likely
relabelings at least as impressive as what we saw — *is the p-value*, and it required no
model of how tea tastes, no Normal curve, nothing but counting equally likely
arrangements.

That is the entire permutation test in one example. The rest of this article is the same
move made general: replace "perfect score" with *any* test statistic $T$, replace
"$\binom{8}{4}$ guesses" with *all relabelings the null treats as equivalent*, and count
the fraction at least as extreme as the observed $T$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-tea
#| fig-cap: 'The lady tasting tea, by enumeration. Under the null ''she is guessing,'' all $\binom{8}{4}=70$ ways of choosing four ''milk-first'' cups are equally likely. The bars show how many of those 70 guesses score $0,1,2,3,4$ cups correct (the hypergeometric null distribution). A perfect score (green) happens in exactly one of the 70 worlds, so the one-sided p-value is $1/70\approx0.014$. No distribution was assumed: the null *built* this histogram by counting equally likely relabelings.'
from math import comb

# Score = number of the 4 truly milk-first cups that fall among her 4 guesses.
# Under guessing, the count of correct guesses k follows a hypergeometric law:
#   P(K=k) = C(4,k) C(4,4-k) / C(8,4).
ks = np.arange(0, 5)
counts = np.array([comb(4, k) * comb(4, 4 - k) for k in ks])  # out of 70
assert counts.sum() == comb(8, 4) == 70

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
bars = ax.bar(ks, counts, color=FILL, edgecolor=DATA, width=0.7)
bars[4].set_color(OBS)
for k, c in zip(ks, counts):
    ax.text(k, c + 0.6, str(int(c)), ha="center", fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlabel("number of milk-first cups correctly identified")
ax.set_ylabel("number of the 70 equally-likely guesses")
ax.set_title("The null distribution, built by counting relabelings")
ax.set_xticks(ks)
ax.text(4, 9, "observed:\nall 4 correct", ha="center", color=OBS, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We now make the intuition precise. The work splits into three parts: pin down what
**exchangeability** means and why $H_0$ supplies it (@sec-exchangeability), define the
**permutation p-value** as a counted fraction (@sec-pval-def), and prove the test it
yields is **exactly level-$\alpha$** (@sec-exactness).

## Exchangeability: the one assumption {#sec-exchangeability}

::: {#def-exchangeable}
**Definition (exchangeability).** Random variables $Z_1,\dots,Z_N$ are **exchangeable** if
their joint distribution is unchanged by any permutation of their indices: for every
permutation $\pi$ of $\{1,\dots,N\}$,
$$
(Z_1, Z_2, \dots, Z_N) \;\overset{d}{=}\; (Z_{\pi(1)}, Z_{\pi(2)}, \dots, Z_{\pi(N)}),
$$
where $\overset{d}{=}$ means "has the same distribution as."
:::

Read the definition slowly. It says: if you shuffle the order of the variables, the
*probability of seeing any particular set of values* does not change. It is strictly
weaker than "i.i.d." — independent and identically distributed variables are
exchangeable (shuffling i.i.d. draws plainly cannot change their joint law), but
exchangeable variables need not be independent. For our purposes the key consequence is
combinatorial and we state it as a lemma because the entire test rests on it.

::: {#lem-equal-prob}
**Lemma (equally likely relabelings).** If $Z_1,\dots,Z_N$ are exchangeable and we observe
the *values* $z_1,\dots,z_N$, then — conditional on the observed *multiset* of values
$\{z_1,\dots,z_N\}$ — every distinct ordering of those values is **equally probable**.
:::

::: {.proof}
Fix the observed multiset of values. Let $\pi$ be any permutation of the indices. By
@def-exchangeable the random vector $(Z_1,\dots,Z_N)$ and its permuted version
$(Z_{\pi(1)},\dots,Z_{\pi(N)})$ have the *same* joint distribution. Therefore, for the
particular ordered tuple $(z_1,\dots,z_N)$ and its rearrangement
$(z_{\pi(1)},\dots,z_{\pi(N)})$,
$$
\Pr\!\big[(Z_1,\dots,Z_N) = (z_1,\dots,z_N)\big]
=
\Pr\!\big[(Z_1,\dots,Z_N) = (z_{\pi(1)},\dots,z_{\pi(N)})\big].
$$
(For continuous data, read $\Pr[\cdot]$ as a density and the same equality holds, since a
density is also invariant under the index permutation.) So *any two orderings of the same
values carry equal probability.* Conditioning on the observed multiset just normalizes
these equal probabilities to sum to one over the distinct orderings, leaving each ordering
with probability $1/(\text{number of distinct orderings})$ — i.e. uniform. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This lemma is the whole engine. It converts the assumption "$H_0$ makes the data
exchangeable" into the operational statement "under $H_0$, every relabeling of the data
is equally likely" — and *equally likely* is exactly what lets us count.

Now, where does $H_0$ supply exchangeability? Two canonical cases, which cover most uses:

**The two-sample case.** We have group A with values $x_1,\dots,x_m$ and group B with
values $y_1,\dots,y_n$, total $N = m+n$. The null is
$$
H_0:\quad F_A = F_B \qquad\text{(the two groups have the *same* distribution).}
$$
If that null holds, then pooling all $N$ observations gives $N$ draws from one common
distribution — and which $m$ of them got the label "A" was arbitrary. Formally, under
$H_0$ the pooled values are exchangeable (i.i.d. from the common $F$), so by
@lem-equal-prob every way of splitting the $N$ pooled values into a group of $m$ and a
group of $n$ is equally likely. There are $\binom{N}{m}$ such splits. *That* is the set we
permute over.

**The independence / correlation case.** We have paired data
$(x_1,y_1),\dots,(x_n,y_n)$ and ask whether $X$ and $Y$ are associated. The null is
$$
H_0:\quad X \perp Y \qquad\text{(}X\text{ and }Y\text{ are independent).}
$$
If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, then *which* $y$ got paired with *which* $x$ was
arbitrary — any of the $n!$ pairings is equally likely, because under independence the
joint density factorizes and the value of $x_i$ tells you nothing about which $y$ it should
sit with. So we hold the $x$'s fixed and permute the $y$'s: the $n!$ permutations of the
$y$-column are the equally-likely relabelings.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does the two-sample null have to be 'same distribution' and not just 'same mean'?"*
Because exchangeability is a statement about the *whole* joint law, not just a moment. If
$F_A$ and $F_B$ have the same mean but different variances, the pooled values are **not**
exchangeable — relabeling changes the joint distribution (a high-variance value is more
likely to have come from the wide group). So the clean, exact permutation null is "the two
distributions are identical." This is not a technicality: it is precisely the crack
through which the test fails when variances differ, the **Behrens–Fisher** problem we
confront honestly in @sec-failure. The honest one-line version: *a permutation test for
"equal means" is only exact if you are willing to assume "equal everything else" under the
null.*
:::

## The permutation p-value {#sec-pval-def}

Let $\mathcal{G}$ be the set of relabelings the null treats as equally likely — the
$\binom{N}{m}$ group-splits in the two-sample case, the $n!$ $y$-permutations in the
correlation case. For each relabeling $g \in \mathcal{G}$, let $T(g)$ be the value of the
test statistic computed on the data *after applying* $g$. Let $T_{\text{obs}} = T(g_0)$ be
the statistic on the original, un-relabeled data (the identity relabeling $g_0$). Write
$|\mathcal{G}|$ for the number of relabelings.

For a statistic where **large values are evidence against $H_0$** (a one-sided,
upper-tail test), the **exact permutation p-value** is the fraction of relabelings whose
statistic equals or exceeds the observed one:

$$
p_{\text{perm}}
\;=\;
\frac{\#\{\, g \in \mathcal{G} : T(g) \ge T_{\text{obs}} \,\}}{|\mathcal{G}|}.
$$ {#eq-pval-exact}

Read @eq-pval-exact against the lady tasting tea: $|\mathcal{G}| = 70$, the statistic
$T$ = "number correct," $T_{\text{obs}} = 4$, and exactly one relabeling achieves
$T(g) \ge 4$, giving $p = 1/70$. The numerator *always* includes the observed data itself: the identity relabeling $g_0$
gives $T(g_0) = T_{\text{obs}}$, and every number satisfies $T_{\text{obs}} \ge
T_{\text{obs}}$, so $g_0$ is counted. That is why
@eq-pval-exact can never return exactly zero — a feature we will see is essential to the
test's exactness, and the seed of the "$+1$" in the Monte-Carlo version.

For a **two-sided** test we judge by *absolute* extremity. If the null makes the statistic
symmetric (e.g. a difference of means under $F_A = F_B$ has a null distribution symmetric
about zero), the clean choice is
$$
p_{\text{perm}}^{(2)}
\;=\;
\frac{\#\{\, g \in \mathcal{G} : |T(g)| \ge |T_{\text{obs}}| \,\}}{|\mathcal{G}|}.
$$ {#eq-pval-two-sided}
We use @eq-pval-two-sided in the worked examples, and we will say explicitly when "extreme"
means "large in absolute value" versus "large in one direction."

## The test is exactly level-$\alpha$ {#sec-exactness}

The signature property — the reason to reach for a permutation test at all — is that it
controls the Type I error rate *exactly*, with no appeal to large samples. We prove it.

::: {#thm-exactness}
**Theorem (exactness of the permutation test).** Suppose that under $H_0$ the relabelings
$\mathcal{G}$ are exchangeable in the sense of @lem-equal-prob — every $g \in \mathcal{G}$
is equally likely to have produced the observed data. Reject $H_0$ when
$p_{\text{perm}} \le \alpha$. Then the probability of rejecting a true $H_0$ is at most
$\alpha$:
$$
\Pr\big[\,p_{\text{perm}} \le \alpha \mid H_0\,\big] \;\le\; \alpha,
$$
with equality whenever $\alpha$ is an attainable multiple of $1/|\mathcal{G}|$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Work conditionally on the observed multiset of values, so that the only randomness left is
*which relabeling the data actually corresponds to*. By @lem-equal-prob, under $H_0$ this
relabeling $G$ is **uniform** on $\mathcal{G}$: each of the $|\mathcal{G}|$ relabelings has
probability $1/|\mathcal{G}|$.

Now study the random variable $p_{\text{perm}}$. Once the multiset is fixed, the entire
collection of statistic-values $\{T(g) : g \in \mathcal{G}\}$ is **determined** — it does
not depend on which $g$ is the "true" one, because relabeling the data and then computing
$T$ produces the same $|\mathcal{G}|$ numbers regardless of where we started. What *is*
random is which of those numbers is the observed one, $T_{\text{obs}} = T(G)$.

Rank the relabelings by their statistic value, largest first (breaking ties by counting
all tied values as "$\ge$"). The p-value @eq-pval-exact assigned to a relabeling $g$ is
exactly
$$
p(g) = \frac{\#\{g' : T(g') \ge T(g)\}}{|\mathcal{G}|},
$$
i.e. its (upper) rank divided by $|\mathcal{G}|$. The event $\{p_{\text{perm}} \le \alpha\}$
is therefore the event that the *true* relabeling $G$ lands among the most extreme
relabelings — specifically those with $p(g) \le \alpha$. Let $k$ be the number of
relabelings satisfying $p(g) \le \alpha$. Because $G$ is uniform on $\mathcal{G}$,
$$
\Pr\big[\,p_{\text{perm}} \le \alpha \mid H_0\,\big]
= \frac{\#\{g : p(g) \le \alpha\}}{|\mathcal{G}|}
= \frac{k}{|\mathcal{G}|}.
$$
It remains to bound $k$. Since $p(g)$ takes values in $\{1/|\mathcal{G}|, 2/|\mathcal{G}|,
\dots, 1\}$ and equals (upper rank)$/|\mathcal{G}|$, having $p(g) \le \alpha$ requires
(upper rank) $\le \alpha |\mathcal{G}|$. In the absence of ties at the boundary, at most
$\lfloor \alpha |\mathcal{G}| \rfloor$ relabelings can satisfy this, so
$k \le \lfloor \alpha |\mathcal{G}| \rfloor \le \alpha |\mathcal{G}|$, giving
$$
\Pr\big[\,p_{\text{perm}} \le \alpha \mid H_0\,\big] = \frac{k}{|\mathcal{G}|}
\le \frac{\alpha |\mathcal{G}|}{|\mathcal{G}|} = \alpha.
$$
The bound holds *conditionally* on every possible multiset of values; averaging over
multisets (each contributing $\le \alpha$) keeps the unconditional rate $\le \alpha$ as
well. Equality is achieved when $\alpha |\mathcal{G}|$ is an integer and there are no ties
straddling the cutoff, so exactly $\alpha|\mathcal{G}|$ relabelings fall in the rejection
set. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Three things are worth pausing on, because they are precisely the points an examiner
probes.

- **No distributional assumption was used anywhere.** The proof never wrote down a
  Normal density, never took a limit, never required $n$ to be large. The only hypothesis
  used was exchangeability under $H_0$ — exactly the Core's claim.
- **The discreteness is real and benign.** The rate is $k/|\mathcal{G}|$, a multiple of
  $1/|\mathcal{G}|$, so for small samples you cannot in general hit $\alpha = 0.05$ *on the
  nose* — the achievable levels are coarse (in the tea example, multiples of $1/70$). The
  theorem says the true rate is $\le \alpha$, i.e. the test is **conservative** when
  $\alpha$ is not attainable, never anti-conservative. That conservatism is the price of
  exactness with discrete data.
- **The observed data is one of the relabelings.** The identity relabeling $g_0$ is a
  member of $\mathcal{G}$ and contributes to both numerator and denominator. Forgetting to
  include it is the classic bug that makes a Monte-Carlo permutation p-value
  anti-conservative — the subject of the next section.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What exactly makes a permutation test 'exact,' and what does that buy you over a
$t$-test?"* Exact means the finite-sample Type I rate is $\le \alpha$ for *every* sample
size, with the only assumption being exchangeability under $H_0$ — no Normality, no
asymptotics (@thm-exactness). The $t$-test's level is exact *only if the data are exactly
Normal*; otherwise it is merely approximate and relies on the CLT, which can be slow for
skewed or heavy-tailed data at small $n$. So the permutation test buys you a guaranteed
level under a much weaker assumption. The catch you must volunteer: "exact level" is a
statement about Type I error under exchangeability — if exchangeability fails (unequal
variances under a means null), the exactness evaporates, as @sec-failure shows.
:::

# From exhaustive counting to Monte Carlo {#sec-monte-carlo}

@eq-pval-exact says: enumerate *all* of $\mathcal{G}$. For the lady's tea that was 70
relabelings — trivial. But $|\mathcal{G}|$ explodes. A two-sample test with $m = n = 15$
has $\binom{30}{15} = 155{,}117{,}520$ splits; with $m = n = 30$ it is about
$1.18 \times 10^{17}$. Enumerating all permutations is hopeless past tiny samples. The
remedy is the same one the bootstrap used: **estimate the exact p-value by random
sampling** — draw $B$ relabelings uniformly at random from $\mathcal{G}$ and count.

## The correct Monte-Carlo estimator (and the "$+1$") {#sec-mc-formula}

Here is the formula, and then the derivation of why it is written the way it is — the
"$+1$"s are not a fudge.

Draw $B$ relabelings $g_1,\dots,g_B$ independently and uniformly from $\mathcal{G}$, and
let $b = \#\{j : T(g_j) \ge T_{\text{obs}}\}$ be how many of those $B$ random relabelings
match or beat the observed statistic. The **Monte-Carlo permutation p-value** is
$$
\hat p_{\text{MC}} \;=\; \frac{b + 1}{B + 1}.
$$ {#eq-pval-mc}

Why $\frac{b+1}{B+1}$ and not the seemingly natural $\frac{b}{B}$? Two complementary
arguments; you should be able to give either.

**Argument 1 — always count the observed data as its own relabeling.** The exact p-value
@eq-pval-exact *always includes the identity relabeling* $g_0$ (the observed data) in its
numerator, because $T(g_0) = T_{\text{obs}} \ge T_{\text{obs}}$. So when we approximate by
sampling, the honest thing is to treat the observed data as a "free" $(B{+}1)$-th draw that
*always* satisfies the $\ge$ condition. That contributes $1$ to a numerator of "successes"
and $1$ to a denominator of "total relabelings considered" — turning $b/B$ into
$(b+1)/(B+1)$. The naive $b/B$ can return $0$ (if no random relabeling beats the observed),
which is impossible for the exact p-value @eq-pval-exact: a p-value of *exactly zero* would
claim the observed data is more extreme than data identical to itself.

**Argument 2 — validity: $(b+1)/(B+1)$ keeps the test exact, $b/B$ does not.** This is the
decisive reason. We show $\Pr[\hat p_{\text{MC}} \le \alpha \mid H_0] \le \alpha$, so the
Monte-Carlo test inherits the exact level of @thm-exactness.

::: {.proof}
Condition on $H_0$ and on the observed multiset, so the true relabeling $G$ is uniform on
$\mathcal{G}$. Consider the $B+1$ relabelings consisting of the observed one $G$ together
with the $B$ independent uniform draws $g_1,\dots,g_B$. Under $H_0$ these $B+1$ relabelings
are **exchangeable among themselves**: $G$ is uniform on $\mathcal{G}$ and each $g_j$ is an
independent uniform draw from the *same* $\mathcal{G}$, so the $B+1$ statistic values
$T_{\text{obs}}, T(g_1), \dots, T(g_B)$ are exchangeable random variables. Therefore the
rank of $T_{\text{obs}}$ among the $B+1$ values is **uniform** on $\{1, 2, \dots, B+1\}$
(ties handled by the $\ge$ convention, which only helps the bound).

Now $\hat p_{\text{MC}} = (b+1)/(B+1)$ is exactly (upper rank of $T_{\text{obs}}$ among the
$B+1$ values)$/(B+1)$: the "$+1$" in the numerator is $T_{\text{obs}}$ counting itself, the
$b$ is how many of the random draws tie-or-beat it, and the "$+1$" in the denominator is
the total $B+1$. Because that rank is uniform on $\{1,\dots,B+1\}$,
$$
\Pr\!\Big[\hat p_{\text{MC}} \le \alpha \,\Big|\, H_0\Big]
= \Pr\!\Big[\text{rank} \le \alpha(B+1)\Big]
= \frac{\lfloor \alpha(B+1)\rfloor}{B+1}
\le \alpha.
$$
So the test that rejects when $(b+1)/(B+1) \le \alpha$ has Type I rate at most $\alpha$,
*for any finite $B$*. The naive estimator $b/B$ does not enjoy this — it omits
$T_{\text{obs}}$ from the pool, breaks the exchangeability of $B+1$ values, and can reject
slightly too often (it is anti-conservative at small $B$). $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The punchline: **the "$+1$" is what preserves the exactness theorem under Monte-Carlo
sampling.** It is not a continuity correction or a smoothing hack; it is the mathematically
correct bookkeeping of the fact that your real data is itself a member of the null's pool
of relabelings.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Never report a Monte-Carlo permutation p-value as $b/B$, and never report $p = 0$.** If
none of your $B$ random relabelings beat the observed statistic, the correct answer is
$\hat p_{\text{MC}} = 1/(B+1)$, *not* $0$. Reporting $0$ claims certainty that no model can
deliver, and using $b/B$ makes the test reject too often. With $B = 9999$ the smallest
reportable p-value is $1/10000 = 10^{-4}$; if you need to resolve smaller p-values, you
must raise $B$ (or, for the far tail, switch to a tail-specialized method). The choice of
$B$ controls **resolution and Monte-Carlo noise**, not validity — the test is exact for any
$B$, but a small $B$ gives a coarse, noisy estimate.
:::

## How many relabelings do you need? {#sec-how-many-B}

Since $\hat p_{\text{MC}}$ is a proportion, its Monte-Carlo error follows the binomial
logic of [INFR-A1]. Writing $p$ for the exact (true) permutation p-value, the count $b$ is
approximately $\text{Binomial}(B, p)$, so the standard error of the *estimate* $b/B$ is
$\sqrt{p(1-p)/B}$. To pin a p-value near the decision boundary $p \approx 0.05$ to within
about $\pm 0.005$ (a relative error small enough not to flip a decision), you need
$\sqrt{0.05 \cdot 0.95 / B} \approx 0.005$, i.e. $B \approx 0.0475/0.000025 \approx 1900$.
In practice people use $B = 9{,}999$ or $19{,}999$ — chosen so $B+1$ is a round number and
the resolution comfortably exceeds the precision anyone reports. @fig-mc-convergence shows
the estimate settling toward the exact value as $B$ grows.

```{python}
#| label: fig-mc-convergence
#| fig-cap: 'Monte-Carlo convergence of the permutation p-value. For a fixed two-sample dataset we draw label-shuffles one at a time and plot the running estimate $(b+1)/(B+1)$ against the number of shuffles $B$. Three independent Monte-Carlo runs (thin lines) all converge to the same exact permutation p-value (dashed red), with the $\pm$ band $\,\widehat p\pm\sqrt{\widehat p(1-\widehat p)/B}$ (shaded) shrinking like $1/\sqrt{B}$. The estimate is noisy at small $B$ and stable by a few thousand; validity, by @thm-exactness, holds at every $B$.'
# One fixed two-sample dataset.
g1 = rng.normal(0.0, 1.0, size=18)
g2 = rng.normal(0.6, 1.0, size=20)   # a real shift, so p is small-ish but not extreme
pooled = np.concatenate([g1, g2])
m = len(g1)
def meandiff(pool, m):
    return pool[:m].mean() - pool[m:].mean()
T_obs = abs(meandiff(pooled, m))

Bmax = 4000
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
runs = []
for run in range(3):
    r = np.random.default_rng(100 + run)
    hits = 0
    est = np.empty(Bmax)
    for j in range(Bmax):
        perm = r.permutation(pooled)
        if abs(meandiff(perm, m)) >= T_obs:
            hits += 1
        est[j] = (hits + 1) / (j + 2)   # (b+1)/(B+1) with B=j+1
    runs.append(est)
    ax.plot(np.arange(1, Bmax + 1), est, lw=1.0, alpha=0.8, color=[DATA, PERM, GREY][run],
            label=f"MC run {run+1}")
# A high-B reference as the 'exact' value.
runs = np.array(runs)
p_ref = runs[:, -1].mean()
ax.axhline(p_ref, color=REJECT, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=f"exact $\\approx$ {p_ref:.3f}")
Bgrid = np.arange(1, Bmax + 1)
band = np.sqrt(p_ref * (1 - p_ref) / Bgrid)
ax.fill_between(Bgrid, p_ref - band, p_ref + band, color=FILL, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlabel("number of random relabelings $B$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"running estimate $(b+1)/(B+1)$")
ax.set_ylim(0, max(0.2, p_ref * 3))
ax.set_title("Monte-Carlo error shrinks like $1/\\sqrt{B}$")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

Three honest examples, each carried end to end. The first is the bread-and-butter
two-sample difference of means; the second tests a correlation by permuting one column; the
third is a *paired* design where exchangeability lives **within** pairs, not across them —
the case practitioners most often get wrong.

## Example 1 — two-sample difference of means, and agreement with the t-test {#sec-ex-twosample}

A small randomized comparison: a cognitive task completed by a **control** group and a
**caffeine** group, with the outcome a performance score (higher is better). The samples
are small ($m = 12$, $n = 13$) and we are unwilling to assume Normality, so we test
$$
H_0: F_{\text{control}} = F_{\text{caffeine}}
\qquad\text{vs.}\qquad
H_1: \text{the distributions differ,}
$$
using the statistic $T = |\bar x_{\text{caf}} - \bar x_{\text{ctrl}}|$ (two-sided, absolute
difference of means). Under $H_0$ the 25 pooled scores are exchangeable, so we relabel by
choosing which 12 of the 25 are "control" — $\binom{25}{12} = 5{,}200{,}300$ splits, small
enough to *enumerate exactly* and confirm the Monte-Carlo answer.

```{python}
#| label: fig-twosample
#| fig-cap: 'Two-sample permutation test of caffeine vs. control on a performance score ($m=12$, $n=13$). **Left:** the two groups (jittered) with their means; caffeine scores higher on average. **Right:** the permutation null distribution of the absolute mean-difference $|\bar x_{\mathrm{caf}}-\bar x_{\mathrm{ctrl}}|$, built from 50,000 random relabelings. The observed difference (green) sits far in the right tail; the shaded red region is the set of relabelings at least as extreme. The fraction there is the two-sided permutation p-value.'
control  = np.array([ 81, 78, 90, 72, 85, 79, 88, 76, 83, 80, 74, 86.])
caffeine = np.array([ 92, 88, 95, 84, 99, 90, 87, 101, 93, 89, 96, 91, 94.])
m, n = len(control), len(caffeine)
pooled = np.concatenate([control, caffeine])
N = m + n

T_obs = abs(caffeine.mean() - control.mean())

B = 50_000
r = np.random.default_rng(7)
Tnull = np.empty(B)
for j in range(B):
    perm = r.permutation(pooled)
    Tnull[j] = abs(perm[m:].mean() - perm[:m].mean())
b = int(np.sum(Tnull >= T_obs))
p_mc = (b + 1) / (B + 1)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
# Left: the two groups.
jit = lambda k: rng.uniform(-0.07, 0.07, size=k)
axL.scatter(np.zeros(m) + jit(m), control,  color=DATA, s=40, alpha=0.85, label="control")
axL.scatter(np.ones(n) + jit(n),  caffeine, color=OBS,  s=40, alpha=0.85, label="caffeine")
axL.plot([-0.2, 0.2], [control.mean()] * 2,  color=DATA, lw=3)
axL.plot([0.8, 1.2],  [caffeine.mean()] * 2, color=OBS,  lw=3)
axL.set_xticks([0, 1]); axL.set_xticklabels(["control", "caffeine"])
axL.set_ylabel("performance score"); axL.set_title("The two groups")
axL.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
# Right: the permutation null.
axR.hist(Tnull, bins=60, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3)
ymax = axR.get_ylim()[1]
axR.axvline(T_obs, color=OBS, lw=2.2, label=f"observed = {T_obs:.2f}")
tail = Tnull[Tnull >= T_obs]
axR.hist(tail, bins=60, range=(Tnull.min(), Tnull.max()), color=REJECT, alpha=0.6)
axR.set_xlabel(r"$|\bar x_{\mathrm{caf}} - \bar x_{\mathrm{ctrl}}|$ under relabeling")
axR.set_ylabel("count"); axR.set_title("Permutation null distribution")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"observed |mean diff|      = {T_obs:.3f}")
print(f"relabelings beating it    = {b} of {B}")
print(f"two-sided perm p-value    = (b+1)/(B+1) = {p_mc:.5f}")
```

The observed gap of about $`{python} f"{T_obs:.1f}"`$ points is matched-or-beaten by only a
handful of the 50,000 relabelings, giving a permutation p-value of about
$`{python} f"{p_mc:.4f}"`$ — strong evidence the groups differ. Now the question every
examiner asks: **does this agree with the $t$-test?**

```{python}
#| label: fig-perm-vs-t
#| fig-cap: 'Permutation null vs. the Normal-theory $t$ null, same data. The histogram is the permutation null distribution of the *standardized* statistic (the two-sample $t$); the red curve is the theoretical $t_\nu$ density the classical test assumes. They nearly coincide here, so the two p-values agree to three decimals — under conditions kind to the $t$-test, the permutation test reproduces it. The permutation test did not *need* the curve; it built its own and happened to match.'
from math import gamma as Gamma

# Standardized (pooled-variance two-sample t) statistic, so we can overlay Student's t.
def tstat(a, b_):
    na, nb = len(a), len(b_)
    sp2 = ((na - 1) * a.var(ddof=1) + (nb - 1) * b_.var(ddof=1)) / (na + nb - 2)
    return (b_.mean() - a.mean()) / np.sqrt(sp2 * (1 / na + 1 / nb))

t_obs = tstat(control, caffeine)
r2 = np.random.default_rng(11)
Tnull_t = np.empty(B)
for j in range(B):
    perm = r2.permutation(pooled)
    Tnull_t[j] = tstat(perm[:m], perm[m:])
p_perm_t = (np.sum(np.abs(Tnull_t) >= abs(t_obs)) + 1) / (B + 1)

# Student-t density (pooled df) drawn by hand from its definition.
nu = m + n - 2
def t_pdf(x, nu):
    c = Gamma((nu + 1) / 2) / (np.sqrt(nu * np.pi) * Gamma(nu / 2))
    return c * (1 + x ** 2 / nu) ** (-(nu + 1) / 2)
# Classical two-sided t p-value via the t CDF (numeric integral of the density).
xs_int = np.linspace(0, abs(t_obs), 2000)
upper_tail = 0.5 - np.trapezoid(t_pdf(xs_int, nu), xs_int)  # P(T > |t_obs|)
p_t_classical = 2 * upper_tail

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(Tnull_t, bins=70, density=True, color=FILL, edgecolor="white",
        linewidth=0.3, label="permutation null of $t$")
xs = np.linspace(-5, 5, 400)
ax.plot(xs, t_pdf(xs, nu), color=REJECT, lw=2.0, label=f"Student $t_{{{nu}}}$ density")
ax.axvline(t_obs, color=OBS, lw=2.0, label=f"observed $t$ = {t_obs:.2f}")
ax.set_xlabel("two-sample $t$ statistic under relabeling")
ax.set_ylabel("density"); ax.set_title("Permutation null matches the $t$ curve here")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"permutation p (t statistic)  = {p_perm_t:.4f}")
print(f"classical t-test p-value     = {p_t_classical:.4f}")
```

The two p-values agree to about three decimals. This is the reassuring half of the
relationship: **under conditions kind to the $t$-test (roughly symmetric, light-tailed
data), the permutation test reproduces it** — Pitman showed the permutation distribution of
the $t$-statistic converges to Student's $t$ as $n$ grows. So you lose nothing by
permuting when the $t$-test was valid; you simply stop *relying* on its assumption. The
interesting half — where they *disagree* — is next.

## Example 1b — where it beats the t-test: heavy tails at small n {#sec-ex-heavy-tail}

The agreement above was on benign data. Now the honest stress test: a *true null* (both
groups drawn from the **same** extremely heavy-tailed distribution — a $t_{1.2}$, whose
tails are so fat that even its *mean* barely exists), tiny samples ($n_1=n_2=8$), and the
question every interviewer wants — *does the permutation test keep its 5% level where the
classical $t$-test does not?* We simulate under $H_0$ and record the actual Type I rate of
each. The interesting failure here is **conservatism**: a single fat-tailed outlier
inflates the pooled variance, shrinks the $t$-statistic, and the Normal-theory $t$-test
quietly under-rejects — buying its "safety" with lost power, on a null distribution that is
simply the wrong shape.

```{python}
#| label: fig-heavy-tail
#| fig-cap: 'Why permute under heavy tails. We simulate under a TRUE two-sample null with both groups i.i.d. from a $t_{1.2}$ distribution (tails so heavy the mean barely exists), $n_1=n_2=8$, over 3000 datasets, and record each test''s actual Type I rate at nominal $\alpha=0.05$. The permutation test on the mean-difference (orange) holds its level almost exactly, because the two groups ARE exchangeable under this null regardless of the tail shape. The classical $t$-test (red) is distorted -- markedly conservative: fat-tailed outliers inflate the pooled variance and shrink the statistic, so its Normal-theory null is the wrong shape and it under-rejects, losing power. Same data, same statistic, different null distribution -- and only the permutation null is correct here.'
from math import erf, sqrt

from math import gamma as Gfun

def pooled_t(a, b_):
    na, nb = len(a), len(b_)
    sp2 = ((na-1)*a.var(ddof=1) + (nb-1)*b_.var(ddof=1)) / (na+nb-2)
    return (b_.mean() - a.mean()) / np.sqrt(sp2 * (1/na + 1/nb))

# Monte-Carlo Type I study: both groups from the same t_{1.2}, so the null is TRUE.
n1 = n2 = 8
nsim = 3000
nu_t = n1 + n2 - 2
sims = np.random.default_rng(2025)
rej_perm = 0
rej_tclassic = 0
for s in range(nsim):
    a = sims.standard_t(df=1.2, size=n1)      # both groups SAME distribution -> null TRUE
    b_ = sims.standard_t(df=1.2, size=n2)
    # permutation p on |mean diff|
    pool = np.concatenate([a, b_])
    T0 = abs(a.mean() - b_.mean())
    rr = np.random.default_rng(10_000 + s)
    B = 499
    hits = 0
    for _ in range(B):
        pp = rr.permutation(pool)
        if abs(pp[:n1].mean() - pp[n1:].mean()) >= T0:
            hits += 1
    if (hits + 1) / (B + 1) <= 0.05:
        rej_perm += 1
    # classical pooled t, two-sided, via t_nu tail
    tval = abs(pooled_t(a, b_))
    xs_i = np.linspace(0, tval, 800)
    cc = Gfun((nu_t+1)/2) / (np.sqrt(nu_t*np.pi) * Gfun(nu_t/2))
    dens = cc * (1 + xs_i**2/nu_t) ** (-(nu_t+1)/2)
    p_t = 2 * (0.5 - np.trapezoid(dens, xs_i))
    if p_t <= 0.05:
        rej_tclassic += 1

rate_perm = rej_perm / nsim
rate_t = rej_tclassic / nsim

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.bar([0, 1], [rate_perm, rate_t], color=[PERM, REJECT], width=0.55)
ax.axhline(0.05, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.6, label="nominal $\\alpha=0.05$")
for i, rt in enumerate([rate_perm, rate_t]):
    ax.text(i, rt + 0.002, f"{rt:.3f}", ha="center", fontsize=11)
ax.set_xticks([0, 1]); ax.set_xticklabels(["permutation\n(mean diff)", "classical\n$t$-test"])
ax.set_ylabel("actual Type I rate"); ax.set_ylim(0, max(0.09, max(rate_perm, rate_t) * 1.4))
ax.set_title(r"Same null ($t_{1.2}$ data, $n=8$): who keeps their level?")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"permutation Type I rate = {rate_perm:.3f}")
print(f"classical t Type I rate = {rate_t:.3f}  (nominal 0.05)")
```

The permutation test sits on $0.05$; the classical $t$-test misses it, because at
$n_1=n_2=8$ the $t_{14}$ reference distribution it assumes (which is only correct when the
*data* are Normal) is the wrong shape for $t_{1.2}$ data, while the permutation null is —
by @thm-exactness — *exactly* right no matter how fat the tails. This is the payoff the
abstract promised: **permutation matches the $t$-test when the $t$-test is valid and beats
it when it is not**, at no cost beyond a few thousand reshuffles. (Note this null *is*
exchangeable — both groups share one distribution — which is why the permutation test is
exact here; contrast @sec-behrens-fisher, where the null is *not* exchangeable and even
permutation needs care.)

## Example 2 — testing a correlation by permuting one column {#sec-ex-correlation}

We have $n = 30$ paired observations of two variables and want to test whether they are
associated. The null $H_0: X \perp Y$ makes the *pairing* arbitrary, so we permute the
$y$-column against a fixed $x$-column. The statistic is the Pearson correlation
$T = r(x, y)$ (two-sided, by $|r|$). Crucially, **we never need the sampling distribution
of $r$** — the messy Fisher $z$-transform of classical theory — because we build the null
by reshuffling.

```{python}
#| label: fig-correlation
#| fig-cap: 'Permutation test of correlation, $n=30$. **Left:** the scatter of $(x,y)$ with the observed Pearson $r$; there is a visible positive tilt. **Right:** the permutation null distribution of $r$, built by shuffling the $y$-column 50,000 times so the pairing is random. It is centered at zero (independence implies no association on average) and roughly symmetric; the observed $r$ (green) sits in the right tail. The two-sided p-value is the fraction of shuffles with $|r|\ge|r_{\mathrm{obs}}|$.'
gcorr = np.random.default_rng(404)        # dedicated generator: reproducible, independent of upstream figures
nC = 30
x = gcorr.normal(0, 1, size=nC)
y = 0.65 * x + gcorr.normal(0, 1.0, size=nC)   # a genuine, moderate association

def pearson(a, b_):
    a0, b0 = a - a.mean(), b_ - b_.mean()
    return np.sum(a0 * b0) / np.sqrt(np.sum(a0 ** 2) * np.sum(b0 ** 2))

r_obs = pearson(x, y)
B = 50_000
rp = np.random.default_rng(21)
r_null = np.empty(B)
for j in range(B):
    r_null[j] = pearson(x, rp.permutation(y))
b = int(np.sum(np.abs(r_null) >= abs(r_obs)))
p_corr = (b + 1) / (B + 1)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.scatter(x, y, color=DATA, s=35, alpha=0.85)
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$")
axL.set_title(f"Observed data, $r = {r_obs:.3f}$")
axR.hist(r_null, bins=60, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3)
axR.axvline(r_obs, color=OBS, lw=2.2, label=f"observed $r$ = {r_obs:.3f}")
axR.axvline(-r_obs, color=OBS, lw=1.2, ls=":")
axR.set_xlabel("$r$ under random pairing"); axR.set_ylabel("count")
axR.set_title("Permutation null of the correlation")
axR.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"observed Pearson r       = {r_obs:.4f}")
print(f"shuffles with |r|>=|r_obs| = {b} of {B}")
print(f"two-sided perm p-value   = {p_corr:.5f}")
```

The null distribution of $r$ is centered at $0$ — under independence, random pairings
produce no systematic correlation — and the observed $r \approx `{python} f"{r_obs:.2f}"`$
sits far enough out to give $p \approx `{python} f"{p_corr:.4f}"`$. Notice what we
*sidestepped*: the exact small-sample distribution of $r$ is genuinely awkward (it depends
on the joint distribution of $X$ and $Y$), and the classical test only gets a clean answer
by assuming bivariate Normality and using the Fisher transform. The permutation test needs
none of that. It also generalizes for free: swap Pearson's $r$ for Spearman's $\rho$,
Kendall's $\tau$, distance correlation, or a mutual-information estimate, and the *exact same
shuffling machinery* delivers a valid test — you only change the line that computes the
statistic.

## Example 3 — a paired design: exchangeability lives within pairs {#sec-ex-paired}

The most instructive example, because the naive permutation is *wrong*. We measure each of
$n = 14$ subjects **before and after** an intervention; the data are paired
$(b_i, a_i)$, and we test whether the intervention shifts the outcome. If you pool all 28
numbers and shuffle the before/after labels freely — the Example-1 recipe — **you have
broken the design**: a subject's before and after values are correlated (some people just
score high), so the 28 values are *not* exchangeable across subjects.

The null that *does* supply exchangeability is "the intervention has no effect, so within
each subject the before/after labeling is arbitrary." That means the valid relabeling is to
independently **flip the sign of each within-pair difference** $d_i = a_i - b_i$ — under the
null, each $d_i$ is as likely to be $+d_i$ as $-d_i$. With $n = 14$ pairs there are
$2^{14} = 16{,}384$ sign patterns, small enough to enumerate exactly.

```{python}
#| label: fig-paired
#| fig-cap: 'Paired permutation test by sign-flipping, $n=14$ subjects. **Left:** before/after pairs joined by lines; most subjects improve. **Right:** the exact null distribution of the mean within-pair difference $\bar d$, built by enumerating all $2^{14}$ ways to flip the signs of the differences (the within-pair exchangeability the null supplies). The observed $\bar d$ (green) is far in the tail. The wrong, pooled relabeling would have given a different and invalid null.'
before = np.array([52, 60, 47, 55, 63, 49, 58, 51, 66, 44, 57, 53, 61, 48.])
after  = np.array([58, 62, 54, 57, 70, 55, 60, 56, 69, 52, 63, 55, 67, 53.])
d = after - before
nP = len(d)
dbar_obs = d.mean()

# Exact enumeration of all 2^n sign flips.
from itertools import product
signs = np.array(list(product([1, -1], repeat=nP)))   # shape (2^n, n)
dbar_null = (signs * d).mean(axis=1)
b = int(np.sum(np.abs(dbar_null) >= abs(dbar_obs)))
p_paired = b / signs.shape[0]   # exact enumeration includes the observed (all +1) row

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
for i in range(nP):
    axL.plot([0, 1], [before[i], after[i]], color=GREY, lw=1.0, alpha=0.7)
axL.scatter(np.zeros(nP), before, color=DATA, s=35, label="before", zorder=3)
axL.scatter(np.ones(nP),  after,  color=OBS,  s=35, label="after",  zorder=3)
axL.set_xticks([0, 1]); axL.set_xticklabels(["before", "after"])
axL.set_ylabel("score"); axL.set_title("Paired measurements")
axL.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.hist(dbar_null, bins=50, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3)
axR.axvline(dbar_obs, color=OBS, lw=2.2, label=fr"observed $\bar d$ = {dbar_obs:.2f}")
axR.axvline(-dbar_obs, color=OBS, lw=1.2, ls=":")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\bar d$ under sign flips"); axR.set_ylabel("count")
axR.set_title(r"Exact null over all $2^{14}$ sign patterns")
axR.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"observed mean difference  dbar = {dbar_obs:.3f}")
print(f"sign patterns as extreme       = {b} of {signs.shape[0]} = 2^{nP}")
print(f"exact two-sided perm p-value   = {p_paired:.6f}")
```

The exact p-value over all $16{,}384$ sign patterns is about
$`{python} f"{p_paired:.4f}"`$ — the intervention shifts the score. This is the
permutation analogue of the **paired $t$-test**, and it makes the design-respecting logic
visible: *what is exchangeable under the null is the sign of each difference, not the
pooled values.* The lesson generalizes to every blocked or stratified design — you permute
*within* the blocks that the null leaves symmetric, never across them.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You have a paired experiment — can you just permute the two columns freely?"* No, and
this is the most common permutation-test mistake. Free permutation assumes the 28 values
are exchangeable, but pairing induces within-subject correlation that pooling destroys —
the free-permutation null is the *wrong* distribution and the test loses its level. The
correct relabeling is whatever the null actually makes symmetric: here, sign-flipping the
within-pair differences (each $d_i \to \pm d_i$). The rule to recite: **permute within the
strata the null treats as exchangeable, and only those.**
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The permutation test's exactness (@thm-exactness) rests entirely on one hypothesis:
exchangeability under $H_0$. The honest treatment is to show where that hypothesis quietly
fails — because when it does, the test keeps producing confident p-values that no longer
mean what they claim.

## Unequal variances under a "means" null: the Behrens–Fisher trap {#sec-behrens-fisher}

The classic failure. Suppose you intend to test *equality of means* but the two groups have
**different variances**. Now the two-sample permutation null ("the labels are
exchangeable") corresponds to $F_A = F_B$ — equal *distributions*, hence equal variances
too. If the variances actually differ, the pooled values are **not exchangeable** under the
means-null, and the permutation test's level is no longer guaranteed. @fig-behrens shows
the damage: a Monte-Carlo study under a *true* null of equal means but unequal variances,
where the permutation test on the raw mean-difference rejects more (or less) than its
nominal 5%.

```{python}
#| label: fig-behrens
#| fig-cap: "The Behrens-Fisher trap. We simulate under a TRUE null of equal means but UNEQUAL variances and unequal group sizes, and record the actual Type I rate of three nominal-5% tests over 2000 datasets. The permutation test on the raw mean-difference (orange) drifts off 5% because equal-means-with-unequal-variances is not exchangeable. The Welch t-test (green) and a permutation test on the Welch-studentized statistic (blue) stay near 5%, because studentizing removes the variance the relabeling cannot see. Exactness is a property of exchangeability, not of permuting per se."
from math import erf, sqrt
def welch_t(a, b_):
    return (a.mean() - b_.mean()) / np.sqrt(a.var(ddof=1)/len(a) + b_.var(ddof=1)/len(b_))

def perm_p(a, b_, statfn, B=1500, seed=0):
    pool = np.concatenate([a, b_]); ma = len(a)
    T0 = abs(statfn(a, b_))
    rr = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    hits = 0
    for _ in range(B):
        pp = rr.permutation(pool)
        if abs(statfn(pp[:ma], pp[ma:])) >= T0:
            hits += 1
    return (hits + 1) / (B + 1)

n_sim = 2000
ma, mb = 10, 30          # unequal sizes amplify the problem
sa, sb = 1.0, 3.0        # unequal variances; means both 0 (null TRUE)
rej_perm_raw, rej_perm_welch, rej_welch = 0, 0, 0
sims = np.random.default_rng(2024)
for s in range(n_sim):
    a = sims.normal(0, sa, ma)
    b_ = sims.normal(0, sb, mb)
    # raw mean-difference permutation
    if perm_p(a, b_, lambda u, v: u.mean() - v.mean(), B=999, seed=s) <= 0.05:
        rej_perm_raw += 1
    # Welch-studentized permutation
    if perm_p(a, b_, welch_t, B=999, seed=s + 99999) <= 0.05:
        rej_perm_welch += 1
    # classical Welch t (two-sided, normal approx to its t df is fine for the rate)
    tv = welch_t(a, b_)
    # Welch df
    va, vb = a.var(ddof=1)/ma, b_.var(ddof=1)/mb
    nu = (va + vb) ** 2 / (va**2/(ma-1) + vb**2/(mb-1))
    # two-sided p via normal tail as a quick stand-in (df large enough here)
    p_w = 2 * (1 - 0.5 * (1 + erf(abs(tv) / sqrt(2))))
    if p_w <= 0.05:
        rej_welch += 1

rates = [rej_perm_raw/n_sim, rej_welch/n_sim, rej_perm_welch/n_sim]
labels = ["perm (raw\nmean diff)", "Welch t", "perm (Welch-\nstudentized)"]
colors = [PERM, OBS, DATA]
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.bar(np.arange(3), rates, color=colors, width=0.6)
ax.axhline(0.05, color=REJECT, ls="--", lw=1.6, label="nominal $\\alpha=0.05$")
for i, rt in enumerate(rates):
    ax.text(i, rt + 0.003, f"{rt:.3f}", ha="center", fontsize=10)
ax.set_xticks(np.arange(3)); ax.set_xticklabels(labels, fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel("actual Type I rate"); ax.set_ylim(0, max(0.12, max(rates) * 1.3))
ax.set_title("Equal means, unequal variances: who keeps their level?")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

for lab, rt in zip(["perm-raw", "Welch-t", "perm-Welch"], rates):
    print(f"{lab:>12}: Type I = {rt:.3f}")
```

Read the bars: the raw-mean-difference permutation test, run on data that are *not*
exchangeable under the means-null, departs from $0.05$ — its "exact" guarantee is void. The
fix is the same one Welch invented for the $t$-test ([INFR-C2], [INFR-D2]): permute a
**studentized** statistic (the Welch $t$, which divides by each group's own standard error)
rather than the raw difference. Studentizing makes the statistic approximately
pivotal — insensitive to the nuisance variances — so the test regains (approximate)
validity even when only the means are equal. The general principle, due to the modern
permutation-test literature:

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Permute a studentized (pivotal) statistic, not a raw one, whenever a nuisance parameter
can differ under the null you care about.** A permutation test is exact only for the null
that makes the data exchangeable — usually "identical distributions." If your real null is
weaker ("equal means," allowing unequal variances), permuting the raw mean-difference tests
the *wrong* null and can be anti-conservative. Permuting the Welch-studentized statistic
restores approximate validity because the studentized statistic does not depend on the
variances the relabeling cannot reproduce. "Permutation test" is not a synonym for
"assumption-free"; it relocates the assumption from *Normality* to *exchangeability*, and
you must check the latter.
:::

## Dependence: exchangeability fails when order matters {#sec-dependence}

Permutation destroys order. If the data are a **time series** — autocorrelated, trending,
or seasonal — then the ordering carries information, the observations are *not*
exchangeable, and shuffling them manufactures a null distribution that is far too narrow
(it scrambles away the dependence, making the data look more informative than it is). The
result is a permutation test that rejects wildly too often.

```{python}
#| label: fig-dependence
#| fig-cap: 'Permutation breaks on dependent data. Two autocorrelated time series ($AR(1)$, $\phi=0.8$) are generated INDEPENDENTLY of each other (true correlation null). The histogram is the permutation null of their correlation, built by shuffling one series; it is far too narrow because shuffling destroys the within-series dependence. The true sampling distribution of the correlation between two independent $AR(1)$ series (red curve, from a proper simulation that regenerates whole series) is much wider. The observed values routinely fall in the permutation tail, so the permutation test fabricates significance that is not there.'
def ar1(nlen, phi, gen):
    e = gen.normal(0, 1, nlen); z = np.empty(nlen); z[0] = e[0]
    for t in range(1, nlen):
        z[t] = phi * z[t-1] + e[t]
    return z

nT, phi = 120, 0.8
gA = np.random.default_rng(5)
# Permutation null: one realization, shuffle one series.
xa = ar1(nT, phi, gA); ya = ar1(nT, phi, gA)
def pear(a, b_):
    a0, b0 = a - a.mean(), b_ - b_.mean()
    return np.sum(a0*b0) / np.sqrt(np.sum(a0**2)*np.sum(b0**2))
perm_null = np.array([pear(xa, gA.permutation(ya)) for _ in range(20000)])
# TRUE null: regenerate whole independent AR(1) pairs and correlate.
gB = np.random.default_rng(9)
true_null = np.array([pear(ar1(nT, phi, gB), ar1(nT, phi, gB)) for _ in range(20000)])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(perm_null, bins=60, density=True, color=FILL, edgecolor="white",
        linewidth=0.3, label="permutation null (shuffle one series)")
ax.hist(true_null, bins=60, density=True, histtype="step", color=REJECT,
        linewidth=2.0, label="true null (independent AR(1) pairs)")
ax.set_xlabel("correlation between the two series")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Permutation null is far too narrow under dependence")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

# Quantify: how often does an independent AR(1) pair land in the permutation 5% tails?
cut = np.quantile(np.abs(perm_null), 0.95)
false_rej = np.mean(np.abs(true_null) >= cut)
print(f"permutation 95% |r| cutoff       = {cut:.3f}")
print(f"true independent pairs beyond it = {false_rej:.3f}  (should be ~0.05)")
```

The printed false-rejection rate is many times the nominal $0.05$: under genuine
independence, autocorrelated series exceed the permutation cutoff far too often. **When you
reach for something else:** for dependent data you must permute in a way that *preserves*
the dependence — **block permutation** (shuffle contiguous blocks, not individual points),
the **block bootstrap** ([INFR-E1] flagged this), or a model-based test that explicitly
accounts for the autocorrelation. Naive permutation is simply the wrong tool when order
carries information.

## What permutation tests cannot give you: a confidence interval {#sec-no-ci}

A subtler boundary. A permutation test answers *"is the effect distinguishable from no
effect?"* — a test of a sharp null. It does **not**, by itself, hand you a confidence
interval for the effect size, because its machinery is tied to the *null* of no effect, not
to estimating a parameter under the alternative. For an interval you turn to the
**bootstrap** ([INFR-E1], [INFR-E2]), which is built around the *sampling distribution of
the estimator* rather than the null's symmetry. (One can invert a *family* of permutation
tests — shift the data by a candidate effect $\delta$ and test the null "effect $= \delta$"
— to obtain an interval, but that requires a location-shift model and is far more work than
a bootstrap interval.) This is the cleanest statement of the division of labor in movement
E: **permutation for testing, bootstrap for intervals.**

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is a permutation test the right tool, and when is it the wrong one?"* The summary
you should be able to recite:

- **Use it** when (a) the null hypothesis supplies a clear **exchangeability** structure
  ("the labels are arbitrary," "the pairing is arbitrary"), (b) you are unwilling to assume
  Normality or the sample is too small for the CLT, or (c) your test statistic has **no
  tractable null distribution** (difference of medians, a custom statistic, an odd
  correlation measure). In that regime it is the most honest test available: exact level,
  distribution-free, and adaptable to any statistic by changing a single line of code.
- **Do not use it** when exchangeability fails: **dependent data** (time series, spatial,
  clustered — permute in blocks or model the dependence instead), or a **nuisance
  parameter that differs under your null** (unequal variances under an equal-means null —
  permute a *studentized* statistic, à la Welch). Do not use it when you actually need a
  **confidence interval** (reach for the bootstrap), and remember its discreteness limits
  the attainable p-values at very small $n$.
- **The deepest caveat:** "permutation test" is often misheard as "assumption-free." It is
  not. It trades the *Normality* assumption for the *exchangeability* assumption — a weaker
  and usually more defensible one, but an assumption all the same, and the entire validity
  of the test rests on it being true.
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You rarely hand-roll the loop.
`scipy.stats.permutation_test` takes your data, your statistic (any callable), and a
`permutation_type` (`'independent'` for two-sample, `'samples'` for paired sign-flipping,
`'pairings'` for correlation) and returns the Monte-Carlo p-value computed with the correct
$(b+1)/(B+1)$ convention; set `n_resamples` to choose $B$ (or request the exact
enumeration when $|\mathcal{G}|$ is small). For correlations, `scipy.stats.pearsonr` and
`spearmanr` accept a `permutation` argument; for the classic small-table case,
`scipy.stats.fisher_exact` *is* the exhaustive permutation test of the lady-tasting-tea
form (@sec-intuition). Internally these do exactly what we did by hand: relabel, recompute
the statistic, count the fraction at least as extreme — and they include the observed data
as its own relabeling, which is why their smallest p-value is $1/(B+1)$, never zero. You
now know what the library is doing and, more importantly, *which* permutation scheme it is
applying and whether that scheme matches the exchangeability your null actually supplies.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-E1 *The Bootstrap*]** (the resampling instinct and the
with/without-replacement contrast) and **[INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*]** (the
null, the statistic, the p-value as tail probability). This article completed the
computational-inference pair by building **exact, distribution-free tests from
exchangeability alone**. From here:

- **[INFR-D5 *Multiple Comparisons*]** uses permutation to control the **family-wise error
  rate** directly: permuting the labels jointly across many test statistics builds the null
  distribution of their *maximum*, giving the **max-T / Westfall–Young** procedure — a
  permutation method that beats Bonferroni by exploiting the correlation among tests.
- **[INFR-D2 *The Workhorse Tests*]** is the assumption-laden counterpart: its $z$, $t$,
  $\chi^2$, and $F$ tests are the fast analytic answers the permutation test reproduces
  under their assumptions and outperforms when those assumptions fail. Several of its
  callouts forwarded here for exactly the small-sample and sparse-count cases.
- **[INFR-E2 *Bootstrap Confidence Intervals*]** is the complement: where permutation tests
  the null, the bootstrap *estimates* and brackets the effect. Together they cover
  assumption-light inference — testing and intervals — end to end.
- **[INFR-C2 *The t-Distribution*]** supplies the Welch correction whose *studentized*
  statistic we permute in @sec-behrens-fisher to survive unequal variances.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** R. A. Fisher, *The Design of Experiments* (1935) — the lady tasting
  tea and the original randomization argument. E. J. G. Pitman, "Significance tests which
  may be applied to samples from any populations" (*JRSS Suppl.*, 1937–38) — the general
  theory and the proof that the permutation $t$ distribution converges to Student's $t$.
- **The modern treatment.** Phillip Good, *Permutation, Parametric, and Bootstrap Tests of
  Hypotheses* (3rd ed.) for breadth; B. Efron and R. Tibshirani, *An Introduction to the
  Bootstrap* (1993), ch. 15, for the bootstrap–permutation relationship; Westfall & Young,
  *Resampling-Based Multiple Testing* (1993) for the max-T procedure that feeds [INFR-D5].
- **On exactness and studentization.** A. C. A. Hoeffding's randomization results and the
  modern result (Janssen; Chung & Romano, 2013) that permuting a *studentized* statistic
  yields asymptotically valid tests even when exchangeability fails for the raw statistic —
  the rigorous backbone of the Behrens–Fisher fix in @sec-behrens-fisher.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How do you permute when the data
  are dependent?* — block and restricted permutation schemes, which preserve the dependence
  the naive shuffle destroys (@sec-dependence). *Can a permutation test give an interval?* —
  yes, by inverting a location-shift family of tests, but the bootstrap ([INFR-E2]) is
  almost always the better route. *What is the relationship to rank tests?* — the
  Mann–Whitney/Wilcoxon and Kruskal–Wallis tests are exactly permutation tests applied to
  the *ranks* of the data, which is why they too are distribution-free.
